questions.title: OOP core concepts
questions.prompt: Name the four main principles of object-oriented programming and give a one-sentence description of each.

markscheme.text: Encapsulation: bundling data and methods together, hiding internal state from outside
Inheritance: a class can extend another, inheriting its fields and methods
Polymorphism: objects of different types can be treated uniformly through a common interface
Abstraction: exposing only essential features, hiding implementation details

questions.title: OOP vs procedural programming
questions.prompt: Compare object-oriented and procedural programming. Give two advantages of OOP over procedural programming, and one disadvantage. Illustrate with a concrete example from modelling a bank account.

markscheme.text: OOP advantage 1: encapsulation — bank account balance hidden behind methods (deposit/withdraw); prevents invalid direct mutation. Procedural: global state accessible to all functions
OOP advantage 2: reuse via inheritance — SavingsAccount extends BankAccount, reusing deposit/withdraw logic while adding interest calculation
Disadvantage: OOP can over-engineer simple problems; class hierarchies add complexity; not always the best fit for algorithmic/mathematical code
Concrete example: procedural — function creditAccount(accounts, id, amount) manipulates a struct; OOP — account.credit(amount) enforces invariants through the object interface

questions.title: Classes vs objects and the class hierarchy
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between a class and an object (instance). What does it mean for Java to be a single-rooted type hierarchy? Draw a small class hierarchy for animals: Animal, Dog, Cat, GoldenRetriever. Mark which relationships are 'is-a' and which are 'has-a'.

markscheme.text: Class: a template/blueprint defining structure and behaviour. Object: a runtime instance of a class with its own state
Single-rooted: every Java class implicitly extends Object; all objects share common methods (toString, equals, hashCode)
Hierarchy: GoldenRetriever extends Dog extends Animal; Cat extends Animal. All are 'is-a' relationships
Has-a: e.g. Dog has-a Collar (composition/aggregation); distinct from inheritance. Is-a is modelled with extends/implements; has-a with fields

questions.title: Compiled vs interpreted languages and the JVM
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain the difference between compilation and interpretation. Where does Java fall on this spectrum?
(ii) What is the JVM? What is bytecode? Why does Java's 'write once, run anywhere' claim depend on the JVM?
(iii) What is JIT compilation and why does it matter for Java performance?

markscheme.text: Compilation: source → machine code ahead of time (e.g. C). Interpretation: source executed line by line at runtime (e.g. Python). Java: hybrid — compiled to bytecode (compile time), interpreted/JIT-compiled by JVM (runtime)
JVM: Java Virtual Machine, a program that executes bytecode. Bytecode: platform-neutral instruction set. Write once, run anywhere: same .class file runs on any JVM, regardless of OS/hardware
JIT: Just-In-Time compiler — JVM detects 'hot' code paths and compiles bytecode to native machine code at runtime. Makes long-running Java programs approach C performance

questions.title: OOP design principles and SOLID
questions.prompt: This question concerns object-oriented design principles.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) and the Open/Closed Principle (OCP). Give an example violating each, and show how to fix it.

markscheme.text: SRP: a class should have only one reason to change. Violation: a Report class that both generates report content AND formats it as PDF. Fix: split into ReportGenerator and PdfFormatter

OCP: open for extension, closed for modification. Violation: a Shape drawing method with if/else for each shape type — adding a new shape requires modifying existing code. Fix: Shape interface with draw(); each shape implements it

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP). Give a classic counterexample involving Square and Rectangle, and explain what LSP violation implies for code correctness.

markscheme.text: LSP: if S is a subtype of T, then objects of type T may be replaced with objects of type S without breaking the program

Square extends Rectangle: Rectangle has setWidth/setHeight independently. Square's setWidth also sets height (to maintain square). Code relying on 'setWidth doesn't change height' breaks when a Square is passed as a Rectangle

LSP violation implies: polymorphism becomes unreliable; you need instanceof checks to avoid bugs; the inheritance hierarchy doesn't model the true is-a relationship (a geometric square is a rectangle, but a Square class is not a Rectangle class in terms of behaviour)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain the Interface Segregation Principle and the Dependency Inversion Principle with examples.

markscheme.text: ISP: no client should be forced to depend on methods it doesn't use. Fat interface violation: Printable has print(), scan(), fax() — a simple printer implements fax() as stub. Fix: separate Printer, Scanner, Fax interfaces

DIP: high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions. Violation: EmailNotifier directly instantiates SmtpClient. Fix: inject a Notifier interface, pass SmtpClient (or SmsClient) at construction

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe composition over inheritance. Give an example where a deep inheritance hierarchy causes problems, and show how composition would be better.

markscheme.text: Composition over inheritance: prefer giving a class references to objects with the desired behaviour, rather than inheriting it. 'Has-a' instead of 'is-a'
Problem: FlyingFishDog needs to extend both Fish and Bird and Dog — multiple inheritance diamond problem. Behaviour explosion: n features → 2^n subclasses

Fix with composition: Animal has a Locomotion field (interface); DogLocomotion, FishLocomotion, FlyingLocomotion implement it. Combine at construction: new Animal(new FlyingLocomotion()) — flexible and avoids class explosion

questions.title: Language comparison: Java, OCaml, Python, C++
questions.prompt: This question concerns programming language design.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Compare static and dynamic typing with examples from Java and Python. What are the trade-offs in terms of safety, expressiveness, and performance?

markscheme.text: Static typing (Java): types checked at compile time; type errors caught before runtime; better IDE support; some loss of flexibility. Dynamic typing (Python): types checked at runtime; more flexible (duck typing); easier prototyping; type errors only caught when code is executed

Safety: static typing catches class of errors (wrong type passed to function) at compile time; dynamic typing may fail at runtime. Java: int x = "hello" → compile error; Python: x = "hello"; x + 1 → runtime TypeError
Performance: static typing allows more optimisation (types known at compile time); dynamic typing requires runtime type checks overhead

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Java uses garbage collection; C++ relies on manual memory management. Give two advantages and two disadvantages of garbage collection compared to manual memory management.

markscheme.text: GC advantage 1: no dangling pointer/use-after-free bugs — GC tracks live objects; memory freed only when unreachable
GC advantage 2: no memory leaks from forgotten deallocations; no need to track ownership manually
GC disadvantage 1: GC pauses — stop-the-world collection causes latency spikes (problematic for real-time systems)
GC disadvantage 2: higher memory overhead (GC needs to track all allocations; cannot immediately reclaim memory); less deterministic performance

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is the difference between pass-by-value and pass-by-reference? How does Java handle primitive types vs objects when passed to methods?

markscheme.text: Pass-by-value: a copy of the value is passed; modifications in the callee don't affect the caller's variable. Pass-by-reference: the reference itself is passed; callee and caller share the same variable
Java is always pass-by-value. For primitives (int, double, etc.): the value is copied — changing the parameter doesn't affect the original

For objects: the reference (pointer) is passed by value. The callee can mutate the object's fields via the reference; but re-assigning the parameter (param = new Foo()) doesn't affect the caller's reference

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: OCaml and Java both have type systems but approach them very differently. Compare how each handles: (i) null/None; (ii) type inference; (iii) immutability.

markscheme.text: (i) OCaml: optional values use type 'a option; the compiler forces you to handle None case. Java: null is a valid value for any reference type — leads to NullPointerException; Java 8+ added Optional<T> but it's not enforced
(ii) OCaml: global Hindley-Milner type inference — most types inferred without annotation. Java: limited local inference (var keyword in Java 10+); generally requires explicit type declarations
(iii) OCaml: immutable by default; mutation requires explicit ref/mutable. Java: mutable by default; final keyword for immutable variables; record types (Java 16+) for immutable data classes

questions.title: Java syntax basics

markscheme.text: Output: 6 5
y = x++: post-increment assigns the current value of x (5) to y, then increments x to 6
After the statement: x=6, y=5. The post-increment ++ operator returns the old value, unlike pre-increment ++x which would assign 6 to y

questions.title: Java types: primitives vs references
questions.prompt: List four Java primitive types and their sizes. Explain the difference between a primitive type and a reference type in Java. What is autoboxing?

markscheme.text: Primitives: int (32-bit), long (64-bit), double (64-bit), boolean (1-bit), char (16-bit Unicode), byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), float (32-bit). Any four correct.
Primitive: stores value directly on stack (or as field inline); no object overhead. Reference type: stores a reference (pointer) to an object on the heap; null is a valid value
Autoboxing: automatic conversion between primitive types and their wrapper classes (int ↔ Integer, double ↔ Double). Allows primitives to be used where objects are required (e.g. in collections)

questions.title: Writing a Java class
questions.prompt: Write a complete Java class Counter with: a private int field count, a constructor initialising count to 0, methods increment(), decrement(), reset(), and getCount(). Include a main method that creates a Counter, increments it 3 times, decrements it once, and prints the result.

markscheme.text: Correct field declaration: private int count;
Correct constructor and methods with appropriate return types (void for increment/decrement/reset, int for getCount)
Correct main method creating instance and calling methods; output should be 2
Proper Java syntax: semicolons, braces, public class name matching file name

questions.title: Arrays and strings in Java
questions.prompt:
(i) Declare and initialise a Java int array of size 5 with values 10,20,30,40,50. Write a method sum(int[] arr) that returns the sum using a for-each loop.
(ii) Explain the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer in Java. When would you use each?
(iii) What does "hello" == "hello" evaluate to, and why might it give surprising results? How should strings be compared?

markscheme.text: Array: int[] arr = {10,20,30,40,50};; sum: int sum=0; for(int x: arr) sum+=x; return sum;

String: immutable; suitable for string constants and few concatenations. StringBuilder: mutable, not thread-safe; best for frequent string building in single-threaded code. StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized); use in multi-threaded contexts at cost of performance
"hello" == "hello" compares references, not content; may be true for string literals (due to interning) but false for dynamically created strings. Always use .equals() for content comparison

questions.title: Java control flow and program structure
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java program structure.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Write a Java method static int[] sieve(int n) that returns an array of all primes ≤ n using the Sieve of Eratosthenes.

markscheme.text: Allocate boolean array isPrime[n+1]; initialise all true; mark 0 and 1 as false
For i from 2 to √n: if isPrime[i], mark multiples i*i, i*i+i, ... as false
Collect all i where isPrime[i]=true into result array; return

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain the difference between break and continue in Java loops. Show an example of a labelled break to exit a nested loop.

markscheme.text: break: exits the innermost enclosing loop (or switch) immediately. continue: skips the rest of the current loop iteration and proceeds to the next
Labelled break: outer: for(int i=0;i<5;i++) { for(int j=0;j<5;j++) { if(i+j==6) break outer; } } — breaks out of both loops when i+j=6

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java? Give one example of each. How are checked exceptions handled?

markscheme.text: Checked exceptions: must be declared in method signature (throws) or caught; represent recoverable conditions. Example: IOException, SQLException
Unchecked exceptions: extend RuntimeException; need not be declared or caught; represent programming errors. Example: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Handling checked: either catch in try-catch block, or declare throws in method signature to propagate to caller

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Java memory model at a high level: what is stored on the stack vs the heap? What is stored where for local variables, method parameters, and object fields?

markscheme.text: Stack: per-thread; stores stack frames containing local variables (primitives stored directly) and references to objects; fast allocation/deallocation (LIFO)
Heap: shared across threads; stores all object instances (their fields) and arrays; managed by GC

Local variables/parameters: primitive values stored on stack; references stored on stack, but the referenced object is on the heap. Object fields: stored inside the object on the heap (whether primitive or reference)

questions.title: Tripos: Java programming
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java programming.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Write a Java class Matrix that encapsulates a 2D array of doubles. Provide a constructor, a get(int i, int j) method, a set(int i, int j, double v) method, and a static multiply(Matrix a, Matrix b) method. Include appropriate bounds checking.

markscheme.text: Correct field private double[][] data; constructor allocating data[rows][cols]
Correct get/set with bounds checking (throw IllegalArgumentException or IndexOutOfBoundsException)
Correct multiply: check a.cols == b.rows; allocate result Matrix; triple nested loop for standard matrix multiplication

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain what happens when you pass an array to a Java method and modify it. Write a method that swaps two elements of an int array in-place, and show it works from main.

markscheme.text: Array passed by reference (reference passed by value): method receives a copy of the reference pointing to the same array. Modifications to array elements affect the original
Swap: static void swap(int[] a, int i, int j) { int tmp=a[i]; a[i]=a[j]; a[j]=tmp; }
Demonstration from main showing array is modified after call

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is method overloading in Java? Write a class Printer with overloaded print methods for int, double, and String. How does the compiler choose which overload to call?

markscheme.text: Method overloading: multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists (number or type of parameters)
Correct overloaded methods: print(int), print(double), print(String) — each with different parameter type
Compiler resolution: selects the most specific overload matching the argument types at compile time (static dispatch). Ambiguity (e.g. null for reference types) causes compile error

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Java compilation and execution process: from .java source file to running program. What roles do javac and java play?

markscheme.text: javac: Java compiler; takes .java source → .class bytecode file(s); performs type checking and compilation
java: JVM launcher; loads .class file; verifies bytecode; interprets or JIT-compiles to native code; executes main method
Class loading: JVM loads classes on demand (lazy loading); resolves class references; bytecode verification ensures type safety before execution

questions.title: Access modifiers
questions.prompt: List Java's four access modifiers and describe what each allows. Which modifier should fields typically use, and why?

markscheme.text: private: accessible only within the same class. (default/package-private): accessible within the same package. protected: accessible within package and subclasses. public: accessible from everywhere
Fields should be private: encapsulation principle — hide internal state; provide access only through public methods (getters/setters) enabling validation and future implementation change

questions.title: Constructors and this
questions.prompt: Explain the role of constructors in Java. What is constructor chaining? Show an example of a class with multiple constructors using this(...) to chain them. What happens if you don't define any constructor?

markscheme.text: Constructor: special method called when creating an object; initialises fields; has same name as class; no return type
Constructor chaining: one constructor calls another using this(...) as first statement. Example: Rectangle() calls this(0,0) which calls this(0,0,1,1)
No constructor defined: compiler inserts a default no-arg constructor that calls super(). If any constructor is defined, the default is not inserted

questions.title: Immutable class design
questions.prompt: Describe how to design an immutable class in Java. List all the steps required. Write an immutable Point class with x and y coordinates. Why is immutability valuable?
questions.hint: Think about what could allow state to change: fields, constructors, methods, subclassing, mutable fields.

markscheme.text: Steps: (1) declare class final; (2) all fields private final; (3) no setters; (4) defensive copies of mutable arguments in constructor; (5) defensive copies of mutable return values
Correct Point class: public final class Point { private final double x, y; public Point(double x, double y){this.x=x;this.y=y;} public double getX(){return x;} public double getY(){return y;} }
Value of immutability: thread-safe without synchronisation; safe to share and cache; simple reasoning (no unexpected state changes); makes good hash map keys

questions.title: Static members and factory methods
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain the difference between instance members and static members. Give two appropriate uses of static fields and two of static methods.
(ii) What is a factory method? Implement a Connection class where the constructor is private and a static factory method create(String url) validates the URL before creating an instance.

markscheme.text: Instance members: per-object (each instance has its own copy). Static members: per-class (shared across all instances). Static field uses: constants (PI, MAX_VALUE), singleton instance, shared counter
Static method uses: utility functions with no state (Math.sqrt), factory methods, main(), operations on the class rather than instances

Factory method: a static method that constructs and returns an instance; can validate input, return cached instances, or return subtype. Connection.create(url): validate url (throw exception if invalid), then return new Connection(url)

questions.title: Class invariants and contracts
questions.prompt: This question concerns class design and correctness.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define a class invariant. Write a Java class BoundedStack with a maximum capacity, maintaining the invariant that size ≤ capacity. Methods: push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull.

markscheme.text: Invariant: condition that is true before and after every public method call. For BoundedStack: 0 ≤ size ≤ capacity
Correct fields: T[] data, int size, int capacity. Constructor validates capacity > 0
push: checks !isFull before adding; pop: checks !isEmpty; invariant maintained by these checks

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe Design by Contract (preconditions, postconditions, invariants). Annotate your BoundedStack with Javadoc @param, @return, @throws comments expressing the contract for push and pop.

markscheme.text: Precondition: what must be true before calling a method. Postcondition: what is guaranteed after. Invariant: always true between calls
push Javadoc: @param item the element to push; @throws IllegalStateException if isFull(); @ensures size increases by 1, peek()==item
pop Javadoc: @throws EmptyStackException if isEmpty(); @ensures size decreases by 1, returns element that was at top; @requires !isEmpty()

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is the difference between defensive programming and design by contract? In what contexts would you choose each approach?

markscheme.text: Defensive programming: method validates all inputs regardless of caller; throws exceptions on invalid input. More robust but adds runtime overhead and duplication
Design by contract: caller is responsible for meeting preconditions; method assumes they hold and does not check (may use assertions in debug mode only). Less overhead; clearer responsibility

Use defensive programming: public API (can't trust callers); security-sensitive code. Use DbC: within a system where contracts can be established; performance-critical code; well-defined component boundaries

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Builder pattern. When is it useful? Write a builder for a Person object with fields name (required), age (optional), email (optional).

markscheme.text: Builder pattern: separates object construction from representation; useful when object has many optional parameters (avoids telescoping constructors or JavaBeans setter issues)

Person.Builder: constructor takes required field (name); optional methods setAge(int), setEmail(String) return this; final build() returns new Person. Fluent API: new Person.Builder("Alice").setAge(30).setEmail("a@b.com").build()

questions.title: Interfaces and abstract classes
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java interfaces and abstract classes.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Compare Java interfaces and abstract classes. Give two things you can do with an abstract class that you cannot do with an interface (pre-Java 8), and two things you can do with an interface that you cannot do with an abstract class.

markscheme.text: Abstract class can: have instance fields (state); have constructors; have protected/package-private methods; implement partial behaviour
Interface can: be implemented by multiple classes (multiple inheritance of type); be used as a pure contract with no state; (Java 8+) have default and static methods

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Design a Java interface Shape with methods area(), perimeter(), and a default method describe() that prints the shape's area and perimeter. Implement it in Circle and Rectangle classes.

markscheme.text: Shape interface: abstract area() and perimeter() returning double; default describe() calling area() and perimeter()
Circle: implements area()=π*r², perimeter()=2π*r
Rectangle: implements area()=w*h, perimeter()=2*(w+h); both provide only the two abstract methods; describe() inherited for free

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is a functional interface in Java? List two examples from the Java standard library. Why are functional interfaces important for lambda expressions?

markscheme.text: Functional interface: an interface with exactly one abstract method (SAM — Single Abstract Method); annotated with @FunctionalInterface (optional but documents intent)
Examples: Runnable (run()), Callable<V> (call()), Comparator<T> (compare()), Function<T,R> (apply()), Predicate<T> (test())

Lambda expressions provide a concise implementation of a functional interface; wherever a functional interface type is expected, a lambda can be used. This enables passing behaviour as a parameter without anonymous class boilerplate

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is the difference between an interface and a marker interface? Give an example of a Java marker interface. What design pattern does it represent?

markscheme.text: Marker interface: an interface with no methods; used to 'tag' classes with a type for runtime type checking or to signal capability. Example: java.io.Serializable, Cloneable
Usage: instanceof checks or reflection; JVM uses Serializable to enable object serialisation. Without the marker, serialisation throws NotSerializableException
Design pattern: represents the Marker pattern; in modern Java, annotations are often preferred (e.g. @Override, @Deprecated) as they are more expressive and don't pollute the type hierarchy

questions.title: References and null
questions.prompt: What is a reference in Java? What is null? What exception does dereferencing null throw? Give a code example that causes this exception.

markscheme.text: Reference: a variable holding the address of an object on the heap (not the object itself). null: a reference value indicating 'no object'
NullPointerException (NPE): thrown when you attempt to call a method or access a field on a null reference
Example: String s = null; int len = s.length(); // NPE

questions.title: Stack and heap allocation

markscheme.text: Stack: local variables x (value 5), s (reference), arr (reference) — all stored in the current stack frame
Heap: String object containing "hello"; int array {1,2,3}
Diagram: stack frame with x=5, s→[String@heap], arr→[int[]@heap]; heap shows two objects with the corresponding data

questions.title: Reference aliasing

markscheme.text: Aliasing: two references point to the same object. Changes via one reference are visible through the other
Output: 99 — b is assigned the same reference as a; b[0]=99 modifies the shared array; a[0] is now 99
Fix: int[] b = Arrays.copyOf(a, a.length); or int[] b = a.clone(); — creates a new array with the same values

questions.title: Object equality vs identity
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain the difference between == and equals() in Java for reference types.
(ii) What is the contract that equals() must satisfy (5 properties)?
(iii) Implement equals() and hashCode() for the following class:
class Point { int x, y; }

markscheme.text: ==: reference equality (same object in memory). equals(): logical equality (contents same). Default equals() uses ==; classes should override for logical equality
equals() contract: (1) reflexive: a.equals(a); (2) symmetric: a.equals(b) ↔ b.equals(a); (3) transitive; (4) consistent; (5) a.equals(null) = false
equals(): check null, same class, then x==other.x && y==other.y. hashCode(): Objects.hash(x,y) or 31*x+y — must satisfy a.equals(b) → a.hashCode()==b.hashCode()

questions.title: Deep vs shallow copy and the Cloneable interface
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java object copying.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define shallow and deep copy. Implement a class Team containing a List of member names. Show that Object.clone() gives a shallow copy. Then implement a deep copy constructor.

markscheme.text: Shallow copy: new object with copied field references; mutable fields are shared. Deep copy: new object with recursively copied mutable fields
Shallow: Team clone has same List reference; modifying clone's list modifies original. Demonstration code showing this
Deep copy constructor: Team(Team other) { this.members = new ArrayList<>(other.members); } — creates new list with same strings (Strings are immutable so no deeper copy needed)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain Java's Cloneable interface and Object.clone(). What are its pitfalls? Why does Effective Java recommend copy constructors or factory methods instead?

markscheme.text: Cloneable: marker interface; implementing it allows Object.clone() to perform field-by-field copy (shallow). Without it, clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException
Pitfalls: clone() returns Object (unchecked cast needed); doesn't call constructors (may bypass invariants); shallow by default; complex for inheritance hierarchies
Copy constructors preferred: explicit, type-safe, can do deep copy, respect invariants, work with final fields, don't require special interface

parts.label: c
parts.prompt:
Java passes object references by value. Explain precisely what happens when you call a method that 'swaps' two references:
static void swap(Object a, Object b) { Object tmp=a; a=b; b=tmp; }

Does this swap the objects in the calling scope? Why or why not?

markscheme.text: The references a and b are local copies of the caller's references. Swapping them only swaps the local copies — the caller's references are unchanged

To observe the swap, the caller would need to inspect a and b after the call — but a and b in the caller are unaffected. Java passes the reference by value: you get a copy of the reference, not the reference itself
This is fundamentally different from pass-by-reference (as in C++ with &): there is no way in Java to write a method that modifies the caller's reference variable itself

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What are Java's strong, soft, weak, and phantom references (from java.lang.ref)? When would you use a WeakReference?

markscheme.text: Strong reference: normal reference; object never GC'd while a strong reference exists. Soft reference: GC may clear when memory is low; useful for caches
Weak reference: GC may clear at any time; object eligible for GC even if only weak references exist. Phantom reference: enqueued after GC for cleanup purposes; get() always returns null
Use WeakReference: for implementing caches where entries should be GC'd when not otherwise referenced (e.g. WeakHashMap); avoids memory leaks in event listener registries

questions.title: Java memory model and concurrency basics
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Java memory model.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Explain what a race condition is. Give a concrete Java example involving a shared counter incremented by two threads. What is the atomic issue with counter++?

markscheme.text: Race condition: outcome depends on the relative timing of concurrent operations; non-deterministic bugs that are hard to reproduce
counter++ is not atomic: read counter, add 1, write back — three separate operations. Two threads can both read 5, both compute 6, both write 6 — result is 6 not 7
Fix: use synchronized, AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet(), or volatile (though volatile doesn't fix compound operations)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain the volatile keyword in Java and the happens-before relationship. When is volatile sufficient and when is synchronisation necessary?

markscheme.text: volatile: ensures visibility — writes to a volatile variable are immediately visible to all threads (no caching in CPU registers). Establishes happens-before: write to volatile happens-before subsequent reads
Sufficient for: simple flags (boolean stop; one writer, many readers); reading/writing single variables atomically (64-bit writes to long/double are otherwise not atomic)
Not sufficient for: compound actions (check-then-act, read-modify-write like counter++); need synchronized or AtomicInteger for those

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the difference between synchronized methods and synchronized blocks. What is the object monitor (lock)?

markscheme.text: Every Java object has a monitor (intrinsic lock). synchronized method: acquires lock on 'this' (or the Class for static methods) for the entire method duration
synchronized block: acquires lock on a specified object for a specific code region — finer granularity, can lock on a dedicated object rather than 'this'
Lock ensures mutual exclusion: at most one thread in a synchronized block on the same object at a time. Other threads block until the lock is released

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is deadlock? Give a minimal two-thread example in Java that deadlocks. State the four Coffman conditions for deadlock.

markscheme.text: Deadlock: two or more threads each holding a lock and waiting for a lock held by another — circular wait, all threads blocked forever
Example: Thread1 locks A then waits for B; Thread2 locks B then waits for A — both block indefinitely
Coffman conditions: (1) mutual exclusion; (2) hold and wait; (3) no preemption; (4) circular wait. All four must hold for deadlock

questions.title: extends and super
questions.prompt: In Java, what does extends do? What is super used for? Write a class Dog extends Animal where Animal has a name field and Dog adds a breed field. Call the Animal constructor from Dog.

markscheme.text: extends: creates a subclass relationship; Dog inherits all non-private members of Animal
super: refers to the parent class. super() calls parent constructor; super.method() calls parent's version of a method
Correct Dog constructor calling super(name) and initialising breed

questions.title: Method overriding and @Override
questions.prompt: Explain method overriding in Java. What is the purpose of the @Override annotation? Give an example where Animal has a speak() method and Dog overrides it. What happens if you call speak() on an Animal reference holding a Dog?

markscheme.text: Overriding: subclass provides its own implementation of a method defined in the superclass (same signature). Dynamic dispatch selects the implementation based on the actual runtime type
@Override: annotation that asks the compiler to verify the method is actually overriding a superclass method; catches typos (if the signature doesn't match, compile error)
Correct example: Animal.speak() returns "..." and Dog.speak() returns "Woof"
Animal ref = new Dog(); ref.speak() → calls Dog's speak() (dynamic dispatch / runtime polymorphism)

questions.title: Abstract classes and the Template Method pattern
questions.prompt: Define an abstract Java class Game with abstract methods initialize(), startPlay(), and endPlay(), and a concrete final method play() that calls all three in order. Implement a Cricket subclass. This is the Template Method pattern — explain how it works.

markscheme.text: Abstract class Game with abstract methods and final play(): public final void play() { initialize(); startPlay(); endPlay(); }
Cricket extends Game, provides concrete implementations of the three methods

Template Method: defines the skeleton of an algorithm in the base class; subclasses fill in specific steps without changing the overall structure. play() is the template; subclasses customise the abstract steps

questions.title: Inheritance vs composition
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain the adage 'favour composition over inheritance'. Give an example where inheritance seems natural but causes problems.
(ii) Show how to redesign the example using composition.
(iii) What does it mean that Java only supports single inheritance of classes but multiple inheritance of interfaces?

markscheme.text: Inheritance creates tight coupling: subclass depends on superclass internals. Fragile base class problem: changes to superclass can break subclasses. Example: Stack extends Vector in Java — inherits irrelevant Vector methods (add at index, set) that break the stack abstraction
Composition redesign: Stack has a Vector field (private); exposes only push/pop/peek. Internal implementation hidden; Vector methods not exposed

Single inheritance of classes: prevents diamond problem for concrete implementations. Multiple inheritance of interfaces: a class can implement many interfaces (pure contracts), enabling flexible type membership without implementation conflicts

questions.title: Constructor chaining and initialisation order
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java initialisation order.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the exact order of initialisation when new C() is called, where C extends B extends A. What gets called first, and in what order do instance initialisers and constructors run?

markscheme.text: Order: (1) static initialisers of A, B, C (in that order, once per class); (2) A's instance initialisers and constructor body; (3) B's instance initialisers and constructor body; (4) C's instance initialisers and constructor body
Within each class: instance field initialisers and instance initialiser blocks run in source order, before the constructor body. super() is implicitly inserted as first statement if not explicit
Trace: new C() → C's constructor calls super() → B's constructor calls super() → A's constructor runs (sets A's fields) → returns to B (sets B's fields) → returns to C (sets C's fields)

parts.label: b

markscheme.text: Output: 0
Explanation: new B() → B() calls super() → A() calls print(). But which print()? Dynamic dispatch: runtime type is B, so B.print() is called
At the time A() runs, B's instance fields haven't been initialised yet (x=42 not yet set); x has its default value 0
This is why calling overridable methods from constructors is dangerous: subclass fields not yet initialised when the method runs

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What does the final keyword mean when applied to (i) a class, (ii) a method, (iii) a variable?

markscheme.text: final class: cannot be subclassed (e.g. String, Integer). final method: cannot be overridden in subclasses
final variable: can only be assigned once (effectively immutable reference); must be initialised in declaration or constructor

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Composite pattern. Design a Java class hierarchy for a file system where File and Directory both implement Component. Directory contains a list of Components.

markscheme.text: Composite pattern: treats individual objects (leaves) and compositions (branches) uniformly through a common interface
Component interface: getName(), getSize(); File implements Component (leaf); Directory implements Component (stores List<Component>)
Directory.getSize(): recursively sums sizes of all children. Client code can call getSize() on any Component without knowing if it's a file or directory — uniformity

questions.title: Tripos: inheritance and design
questions.prompt: This question concerns inheritance in object-oriented design.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Design a Java class hierarchy for a simple expression evaluator supporting integers, addition, and multiplication. Use abstract class Expr with subclasses Num, Add, and Mul. Implement eval() in each.

markscheme.text: abstract class Expr { abstract int eval(); }
Num: field int value; eval() returns value. Add: fields Expr left, right; eval() returns left.eval()+right.eval()
Mul: same structure as Add but with *. Correct Java syntax and constructors

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Extend your expression evaluator to support a print() method that returns the expression as a String (e.g. Add(Mul(2,3),4) → "2*3+4"). How do you handle operator precedence in the string representation?

markscheme.text: Num.print() = String.valueOf(value). Mul.print() = left.print()+"*"+right.print()

Add.print(): need to add parentheses around Mul sub-expressions? No — Mul has higher precedence. Add(Mul(2,3),4) → "2*3+4" without parens. Add sub-expressions of Add need no parens (same precedence, left-assoc)
Rule: Mul needs to parenthesise Add children: if (child instanceof Add) wrap with parens. Add(Add(1,2),3) → "1+2+3" (fine); Mul(Add(1,2),3) → "(1+2)*3"

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: The Visitor pattern is an alternative to adding methods to each subclass. Describe the Visitor pattern and implement an ExprVisitor interface with methods visitNum, visitAdd, visitMul. Show how eval and print could be implemented as visitors.

markscheme.text: Visitor: define operations on an object structure without modifying the classes. ExprVisitor interface: visitNum(Num), visitAdd(Add), visitMul(Mul) — each returns a value (use generic <T>)
Each Expr subclass has accept(ExprVisitor v): Num.accept(v) calls v.visitNum(this), etc.
EvalVisitor implements ExprVisitor: visitNum returns value; visitAdd recursively evaluates left and right via accept

Advantage: adding a new operation (e.g. optimise) requires only a new Visitor class, not modifying all Expr subclasses (OCP). Disadvantage: adding a new Expr subclass requires modifying all Visitor interfaces

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Compare the inheritance-based (eval method in each class) and visitor-based approaches for the expression evaluator. When would you choose each?

markscheme.text: Inheritance approach: open for adding new node types (just add a subclass); closed for adding new operations (must modify all classes). Good when: node types stable, operations vary

Visitor approach: open for adding new operations; closed for new node types. Good when: operations vary frequently, node types stable. The expression problem: can't easily be open for both simultaneously in OOP
Functional languages (e.g. OCaml) solve this differently: pattern matching handles new operations naturally; new types require changing all pattern match sites — dual trade-off

questions.title: Runtime polymorphism
questions.prompt: What is runtime polymorphism in Java? Explain with an example involving an Animal array holding Dog and Cat objects, each with a speak() method. What mechanism enables this behaviour?

markscheme.text: Runtime polymorphism: the same method call on different objects produces different behaviour at runtime; the method called is determined by the actual type of the object, not the declared type of the reference
Example: Animal[] animals = {new Dog(), new Cat()}; for(Animal a: animals) a.speak() — calls Dog.speak() and Cat.speak() respectively
Dynamic dispatch: the JVM uses a virtual method table (vtable) per class to resolve method calls at runtime

questions.title: Coupling and cohesion
questions.prompt: Define coupling and cohesion. What is the desired level of each for well-designed software? Give an example of high coupling that should be refactored.

markscheme.text: Coupling: degree of dependency between modules. Cohesion: degree to which elements within a module belong together. Goal: low coupling (independent modules), high cohesion (focused modules)

High coupling example: class OrderProcessor directly instantiates EmailNotifier, DatabaseLogger, and PaymentGateway — changes to any of these require changing OrderProcessor. Fix: inject abstractions (interfaces) via constructor

questions.title: Java default methods and the diamond problem
questions.prompt: Java 8 introduced default methods on interfaces. Describe the diamond problem that arises when a class implements two interfaces with the same default method. How does Java resolve it? Give a code example.

markscheme.text: Diamond problem: if InterfaceA and InterfaceB both have default void greet() {}, and class C implements both, it's ambiguous which greet() to use

Java resolution rules: (1) class methods win over interface defaults; (2) more specific interface wins (if one interface extends the other); (3) otherwise, class must explicitly override and specify which to use
Code example: class C implements A, B { @Override public void greet() { A.super.greet(); } } — explicitly delegates to A's default

questions.title: Type casting and instanceof
questions.prompt:
(i) What is upcasting and downcasting in Java? Which is safe and which can throw an exception?
(ii) Show an example of incorrect downcasting causing ClassCastException.
(iii) Describe the pattern-matching instanceof (Java 16+) and how it improves safety.
(iv) What is the difference between compile-time type and runtime type?

markscheme.text: Upcasting: Dog → Animal (always safe, implicit). Downcasting: Animal → Dog (requires explicit cast; throws ClassCastException if actual type is wrong)
Example: Animal a = new Cat(); Dog d = (Dog) a; // ClassCastException at runtime — a is a Cat, not a Dog
Pattern-matching instanceof (Java 16+): if (a instanceof Dog d) { d.fetch(); } — binds d only if the cast succeeds; cleaner than cast after check

Compile-time type: the declared type of the variable (used for method resolution at compile time). Runtime type: the actual type of the object created with new (used for dynamic dispatch and instanceof)

questions.title: Polymorphism in practice: Strategy pattern
questions.prompt: This question concerns the use of polymorphism.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the Strategy pattern. Implement a sorting strategy system: a Sorter class that takes a SortStrategy interface and uses it to sort an int array. Provide two strategies: BubbleSort and QuickSort.

markscheme.text: Strategy: defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, makes them interchangeable. Separates algorithm from context
SortStrategy interface: void sort(int[] arr). Sorter holds a SortStrategy field; sort(int[] arr) delegates to strategy.sort(arr)
BubbleSort and QuickSort implement SortStrategy; Sorter can switch strategies at runtime

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: How does the Strategy pattern relate to polymorphism and loose coupling? Compare it to using a switch statement to choose algorithm.

markscheme.text: Strategy uses polymorphism: Sorter calls strategy.sort() — different behaviour depending on which SortStrategy is injected. No casting or instanceof needed
Loose coupling: Sorter depends on the SortStrategy interface, not concrete implementations. New algorithms added without modifying Sorter (OCP)
Switch: requires modifying the switch statement to add new algorithms; violates OCP; Sorter must know all algorithms at compile time. Strategy: open for extension, closed for modification

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe parametric polymorphism (generics) vs subtype polymorphism (inheritance). Give an example showing when each is appropriate.

markscheme.text: Subtype polymorphism: runtime behaviour depends on object type (dynamic dispatch). Animal.speak() dispatched to Dog.speak() or Cat.speak(). Use when: objects of different types need to be treated uniformly with different behaviours

Parametric polymorphism (generics): same code works for any type parameter. List<T> works for List<Integer>, List<String> etc. — same structure, different element types. Use when: container/algorithm independent of element type
Example: List<Animal> — parametric poly for the container; forEach(a -> a.speak()) — subtype poly for the method call. They complement each other

parts.label: d

markscheme.text: obj.s(): static method — resolved at compile time using declared type A. Prints "A.s". Static methods are not polymorphic (hidden, not overridden)
obj.i(): instance method — dynamic dispatch uses runtime type B. Prints "B.i"
Key distinction: overriding is runtime polymorphism; hiding is compile-time selection. @Override does not apply to static methods

questions.title: Interface-based design and dependency injection
questions.prompt: This question concerns interface-based programming and coupling.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe dependency injection (DI). Show constructor injection for a UserService that depends on a UserRepository interface. How does DI improve testability?

markscheme.text: DI: instead of class creating its own dependencies, they are provided (injected) from outside. Inversion of control: caller controls dependencies rather than the object itself
UserService(UserRepository repo) — repository injected at construction; UserService uses interface methods only
Testability: inject a mock/stub UserRepository in tests without hitting a real database; test UserService logic in isolation

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the Observer pattern. Implement it with EventSource (publisher) and EventListener (subscriber) interfaces. Show three listeners registered on an EventSource.

markscheme.text: Observer: subject maintains a list of observers; notifies all when state changes. Decouples publisher from subscribers
EventListener interface: void onEvent(Event e). EventSource: addListener, removeListener, notifyListeners(Event e) iterates and calls onEvent
Three listeners registered; notifyListeners iterates and calls each — all three respond to one event. Java's built-in event system (Swing, PropertyChangeSupport) uses this pattern

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is the Proxy pattern? Describe three use cases (lazy loading, access control, remote proxy). Show a simplified access-control proxy for a DatabaseConnection interface.

markscheme.text: Proxy: provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access, add behaviour, or defer creation
Use cases: lazy loading (create expensive object only when first accessed); access control (check permissions before forwarding); remote proxy (represent remote object via network)

AccessControlProxy implements DatabaseConnection; holds a real DatabaseConnection and a UserRole; execute(query) checks role before calling real.execute(query); throws SecurityException if unauthorised

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Explain the concept of covariance and contravariance in Java. How do they apply to method return types and parameter types when overriding? What is the role of wildcards in generics?

markscheme.text: Covariance: subtype can be used where supertype expected. In Java, overriding method may return a subtype (covariant return type). E.g. Animal.clone() returns Animal; Dog.clone() can return Dog

Contravariance: method parameter types in overriding cannot be narrowed (Java doesn't support contravariant parameters in overriding — would need a different signature, which is overloading). Interfaces can be used contravariantly in general
Wildcards: List<? extends Animal> — covariant (read only); List<? super Dog> — contravariant (write). Get/Put principle: use extends for reading, super for writing

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: Object lifecycle
questions.prompt: Describe the lifecycle of a Java object from creation to garbage collection. When does an object become eligible for GC?

markscheme.text: Creation: new allocates object on heap, calls constructor, returns reference. Object is live while reachable from GC roots (local variables, static fields, JNI references)

Eligible for GC: when no strong references exist — object unreachable from GC roots. Circular references between two unreachable objects: both collected (modern GC uses reachability, not reference counting)
GC runs periodically; object may have finalize() called before collection (deprecated). finalizer execution is not guaranteed or timely — resource cleanup should use try-with-resources

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: Mark and sweep and generational GC
questions.prompt: Explain the mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithm. What is generational garbage collection and why is it efficient?

markscheme.text: Mark-and-sweep: (1) mark phase — trace all objects reachable from GC roots, marking them live; (2) sweep phase — scan the whole heap, reclaim unmarked (unreachable) objects
Generational GC: based on the weak generational hypothesis — most objects die young. Divide heap into young (Eden, survivor spaces) and old (tenured) generations

Efficiency: minor GC (young generation) is fast — small area, most objects collected quickly. Long-lived objects promoted to old gen; major GC (old gen) less frequent. Objects rarely cross generations

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: try-with-resources and AutoCloseable
questions.prompt: Explain Java's try-with-resources statement. What interface must a resource implement? Show the equivalent code using try-finally. Explain why relying on GC for resource cleanup (files, connections) is wrong.

markscheme.text: try-with-resources: try (Resource r = new Resource()) { ... } — automatically calls r.close() on exit (even if exception thrown). Resource must implement AutoCloseable (or Closeable)
Equivalent try-finally: open resource before try; in finally block, check not null and close. try-with-resources is cleaner and handles suppressed exceptions correctly
GC is non-deterministic — may not run until program exits. File handles, DB connections are limited OS resources; holding them indefinitely causes resource exhaustion. Must close explicitly

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: Memory leaks in Java
questions.prompt:
(i) Can Java have memory leaks despite GC? Explain with two examples.
(ii) What is an interned String? How can incorrect use of String.intern() cause memory issues?
(iii) Describe a memory leak caused by listeners not being deregistered.

markscheme.text: Yes: memory leaks occur when objects are reachable but never used. Example 1: static List that elements are added to but never removed — all elements remain reachable. Example 2: caches without eviction policy that grow unboundedly
Interned strings: String.intern() places strings in the string pool (permgen/metaspace); interning many distinct strings fills the pool and triggers OOM. The pool is not GC'd (pre-Java 7)

Listener leak: add listeners to EventSource without removing them when component is destroyed; EventSource holds strong references to listeners, keeping listeners and all their references alive indefinitely

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: GC algorithms comparison
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java garbage collection algorithms.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Compare the Serial, Parallel, CMS (Concurrent Mark Sweep), and G1 garbage collectors. For each, state its main design goal and primary use case.

markscheme.text: Serial: single-threaded stop-the-world; simple; best for single-core or small heaps (client VMs)
Parallel (Throughput GC): multiple threads for GC; maximises throughput; some long pauses; good for batch processing
CMS: concurrent marking phase while application runs; low pause times; fragmentation over time. G1: region-based; predictable pause times; replaces CMS for large heaps; default in Java 9+

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain compaction as a phase in garbage collection. Why is it important? What is the trade-off between compacting and non-compacting collectors?

markscheme.text: Compaction: after reclaiming unreachable objects, slide live objects together to eliminate fragmentation; update all references
Important: prevents fragmentation (many small free gaps unable to satisfy large allocations); allows pointer-bump allocation (very fast — just increment a pointer)

Trade-off: compaction requires stop-the-world (cannot move objects while application threads run); non-compacting collectors (CMS) avoid pauses but fragment the heap, requiring expensive free-list allocation

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is escape analysis in the JVM? How can it reduce GC pressure?

markscheme.text: Escape analysis: JIT determines whether an object's reference 'escapes' the method it was created in — i.e., whether it is stored in a heap-accessible location (field, returned, passed to another thread)
If object does not escape: JVM can allocate it on the stack (stack allocation) — automatically freed when method returns, no GC involvement

Also enables scalar replacement: decompose object into individual fields allocated in registers/stack; no heap allocation at all. Reduces GC pressure for short-lived objects common in functional-style Java code

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the GC root types in the JVM. Why do they form the starting points for reachability analysis?

markscheme.text: GC roots: objects guaranteed to be live without further analysis. Types: (1) local variables and method parameters on all thread stacks; (2) static fields of loaded classes; (3) JNI references (native code); (4) objects held by JVM internals (e.g. class loaders, finalizer queue)

Starting from roots guarantees correctness: any object reachable from a root is by definition live (someone could use it). Unreachable from roots = definitely dead. The GC traverses the object graph from roots, marking all reached objects

questions.topicTitle: Object Life Cycle, Garbage Collection, and Copying
questions.title: Object serialization and the Prototype pattern
questions.prompt: This question concerns object copying and serialisation.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the Prototype pattern. How does it relate to object cloning? Implement a Shape prototype registry in Java that maps shape names to prototype objects and returns copies on request.

markscheme.text: Prototype: create new objects by copying (cloning) a prototype. Avoids expensive creation by reusing a configured prototype
Registry: Map<String, Shape> prototypes; register(name, shape); get(name): return (Shape) prototypes.get(name).clone()
Shapes implement Cloneable and override clone(); clients get fresh copies from the registry without knowing the concrete class

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe Java serialisation (java.io.Serializable). What fields are excluded from serialisation? How can you customise the process with writeObject/readObject? What security concerns does serialisation raise?

markscheme.text: Serialisation: converts object graph to byte stream; deserialistion reverses it. Class must implement Serializable. transient fields excluded; static fields excluded
Customisation: private void writeObject(ObjectOutputStream s) and readObject(ObjectInputStream s) methods can add custom logic (e.g. encrypt sensitive fields, validate invariants on deserialisation)

Security: deserialisation of untrusted data is dangerous — attacker-controlled byte stream can instantiate arbitrary classes and call arbitrary methods (gadget chains). Critical vulnerability in Java; avoid deserialising untrusted data; use ObjectInputFilter; prefer JSON/XML with schema validation

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain serialVersionUID. What happens if you serialise an object, change the class, and try to deserialise the old bytes? How should you handle class evolution in serialised objects?

markscheme.text: serialVersionUID: a version identifier stored in the serialised stream; compared to the class's UID on deserialisation. If they don't match, InvalidClassException is thrown
Adding fields: declare compatible serialVersionUID; added fields receive default values on deserialisation from old stream. Removing fields: data silently ignored

Best practice: explicitly declare serialVersionUID; use readObject to validate/migrate; consider versioned serialisation protocols or replace with schema-based formats (JSON, Protobuf) for better evolution support

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Records (Java 16+) are immutable data classes. How do they differ from regular classes? How do they interact with serialisation and the equals/hashCode contract?

markscheme.text: Records: final class; all fields private final; compiler generates constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, toString automatically based on components
Records implement Serializable (if declared): serialisation uses the canonical constructor on deserialisation (not field-by-field); safer than regular Serializable
equals/hashCode: generated based on all components; two records with same component values are equal. Cannot be subclassed (implicitly final) — avoids equals contract violations from inheritance

questions.title: Java Collections overview
questions.prompt: Name four Java collection interfaces and a concrete implementation of each. When would you choose ArrayList over LinkedList?

markscheme.text: List (ArrayList, LinkedList); Set (HashSet, TreeSet); Map (HashMap, TreeMap); Queue (LinkedList, ArrayDeque)
ArrayList over LinkedList: random access O(1) vs O(n); better cache locality; uses less memory (no node overhead). LinkedList better for frequent insertions/deletions in the middle

questions.title: Comparable and Comparator
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between Comparable and Comparator. When would you use each? Write a Student class implementing Comparable ordering by GPA. Then write a Comparator that orders by name.

markscheme.text: Comparable: natural ordering defined within the class (compareTo). Comparator: external ordering; useful when you don't own the class or need multiple orderings
Student implements Comparable<Student>: compareTo compares GPA (Double.compare(this.gpa, other.gpa))
Name comparator: Comparator byName = Comparator.comparing(s -> s.name); or explicit lambda/anonymous class
Usage: Collections.sort(list) uses natural order; Collections.sort(list, comparator) uses provided comparator

questions.title: HashMap internals
questions.prompt: Explain how HashMap works internally in Java. What is the role of hashCode() and equals()? What happens when two keys have the same hash code? What is the default load factor and why does it matter?
questions.hint: Think about how keys are stored, what happens at the same bucket, and when rehashing occurs.

markscheme.text: HashMap: array of buckets; key mapped to bucket via hashCode() % capacity. equals() used to find exact key within a bucket (handles collisions)
Collision (same hash): keys stored in a linked list (or balanced tree for Java 8+ when bucket length > 8) at the same bucket. Lookup traverses the chain
Load factor (default 0.75): when size/capacity > 0.75, rehash (double capacity, reinsert all entries). Too low: wasted space. Too high: long chains, slower lookup. 0.75 balances space/time

questions.title: TreeMap, TreeSet, and ordering
questions.prompt:
(i) How does TreeMap differ from HashMap in terms of ordering and performance?
(ii) Write code to count word frequencies using a TreeMap, printing results in alphabetical order.
(iii) What happens if you put a mutable object as a HashMap key and then mutate it?

markscheme.text: TreeMap: sorted by key (natural order or Comparator); O(log n) operations (red-black BST). HashMap: unordered; O(1) amortised operations. Use TreeMap when sorted iteration needed
Word count: Map<String,Integer> map = new TreeMap<>(); for each word: map.merge(word, 1, Integer::sum); forEach prints in alphabetical order

Mutable key bug: hash computed at put time; if key's hash changes after mutation, the entry cannot be found at the new bucket. The entry is 'lost' — get() returns null even though the key is 'in' the map. HashMap keys should be immutable

questions.title: Collections algorithms and complexity
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java collections and algorithms.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Explain the Iterator pattern. How does Java's for-each loop relate to Iterable<T> and Iterator<T>? Implement an Iterable range class Range(int from, int to) that can be used in a for-each loop.

markscheme.text: Iterator pattern: provides a way to sequentially access elements of a collection without exposing internal representation. Iterator<T>: hasNext(), next()
for-each on Iterable: compiler desugars to Iterator loop; Iterable<T>.iterator() returns an Iterator<T>
Range: implements Iterable<Integer>; iterator() returns a new Iterator that tracks current position from 'from' to 'to'-1

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the time complexity of common operations (add, remove, contains) for ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, and TreeSet.

markscheme.text: ArrayList: add end O(1) amortised, O(n) worst; remove by index O(n) shift; contains O(n)
LinkedList: add/remove at ends O(1); add/remove by index O(n) traversal; contains O(n)
HashSet: add/remove/contains O(1) amortised (O(n) worst case — all in one bucket). TreeSet: all O(log n) — balanced BST

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is a PriorityQueue in Java? What ordering does it use by default? Show how to use a custom Comparator to create a max-heap priority queue.

markscheme.text: PriorityQueue: heap-based; extractMin is O(log n). Default: natural ordering (Comparable) — min-heap (smallest element at head)
Max-heap: PriorityQueue maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.reverseOrder());
Operations: add(e) O(log n); poll() (extract min/max) O(log n); peek() O(1); contains O(n) — not indexed. Does not support O(log n) remove by value or decrease-key

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Java's Collections.sort is stable (since Java 7). Why does stability matter when sorting a list of Student objects first by grade, then by name? Show the sorting code.

markscheme.text: Sort by name first, then stable sort by grade: the name ordering is preserved among students with equal grades. Without stability, the secondary name ordering would be lost
Code: students.sort(Comparator.comparing(Student::getName)); students.sort(Comparator.comparing(Student::getGrade)); — stable sort preserves name order within each grade
Alternatively: students.sort(Comparator.comparing(Student::getGrade).thenComparing(Student::getName)); — single sort with compound comparator; cleaner and avoids needing stability

questions.title: Tripos: data structures in Java
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java data structures.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Implement a generic Stack<T> in Java backed by a resizing array (like ArrayList). Include push, pop, peek, isEmpty, and size. Ensure proper type safety. Explain the unchecked cast required when creating the backing array.

markscheme.text: Correct generic class with T[] data; constructor allocates (T[]) new Object[capacity] — unchecked cast required because Java cannot create generic arrays directly
Correct push with resizing (double capacity when full); pop decrements size (and possibly shrinks array); peek returns top without removing

Unchecked cast explanation: type erasure — T is erased to Object at runtime; (T[]) new Object[n] is technically unsound but safe as long as the array is not exposed raw. @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") appropriate here

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: What is a LinkedHashMap in Java? How does it differ from HashMap and TreeMap? Give a use case where it is the ideal choice.

markscheme.text: LinkedHashMap: HashMap with a doubly-linked list maintaining insertion order (or access order if accessOrder=true). O(1) operations like HashMap but iteration is in insertion order
Vs HashMap: maintains order. Vs TreeMap: O(1) operations vs O(log n); insertion order not sorted order
Use case: LRU cache — LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true; override removeEldestEntry to evict oldest accessed entry when size exceeds limit. Only a few lines of code needed

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe ConcurrentHashMap. How does it achieve thread safety without a global lock? Compare to using Collections.synchronizedMap.

markscheme.text: ConcurrentHashMap: divides map into segments (Java 7) or uses CAS/lock striping on individual buckets (Java 8). Multiple threads can read/write different segments concurrently
Vs synchronizedMap: synchronizedMap wraps each operation in a single lock — all threads serialised. ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads (lock-free) and fine-grained writes
Caveat: compound actions (check-then-act) on ConcurrentHashMap are not atomic unless using its atomic methods (computeIfAbsent, putIfAbsent, merge). Use these to avoid races

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Deque interface. How can it be used as both a stack and a queue? Give the methods for each use case.

markscheme.text: Deque (double-ended queue): supports insertion and removal at both ends. ArrayDeque is the preferred implementation (faster than LinkedList for most uses)
As stack: push (addFirst), pop (removeFirst), peek (peekFirst) — LIFO. Preferred over java.util.Stack (which is synchronized and has legacy design issues)
As queue: offer (addLast), poll (removeFirst), peek (peekFirst) — FIFO. Deque unifies both interfaces; choose the usage pattern at the call site

questions.title: Generics basics
questions.prompt: What problem do Java generics solve? Write a generic Pair class with getters. What is type erasure?

markscheme.text: Generics solve: eliminate ClassCastExceptions from unsafe casts; provide compile-time type safety for containers without duplication of code for each type
Pair<A,B>: private final A first; private final B second; constructor; getFirst/getSecond. Type parameters A and B are placeholders

Type erasure: at runtime, generic type parameters are erased to their bounds (usually Object). A List<String> and List<Integer> are the same List class at runtime. Enables backward compatibility with pre-generics code

questions.title: Bounded type parameters
questions.prompt: Explain bounded type parameters in Java generics. Write a generic method max(T[] arr) that returns the maximum element, using T extends Comparable. What does the bound enable?

markscheme.text: Bounded type parameter: restricts the type variable to a subtype of a given bound. <T extends Comparable<T>>: T must implement Comparable — allows calling compareTo on T values
static > T max(T[] arr) { T m=arr[0]; for(T x:arr) if(x.compareTo(m)>0) m=x; return m; }
The bound enables: calling compareTo on T instances (wouldn't compile without it since Object doesn't have compareTo)

questions.title: Wildcards: ? extends and ? super
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between List<? extends Number> and List<? super Integer>. For each, state what you can read from and write to the list. Give a use case for each.

markscheme.text: ? extends Number: upper-bounded wildcard. Can read elements as Number (or Object). Cannot add elements (type is unknown — could be List, adding Integer would be wrong). Use for: reading from a collection of numbers (summing elements)
? super Integer: lower-bounded wildcard. Can add Integer (or subtype). Can only read as Object. Use for: writing Integers to a collection (e.g. copying Integers into a List or List
PECS principle: Producer Extends, Consumer Super — use extends when only reading from collection, super when only writing

questions.title: Generic methods and type inference
questions.prompt:
(i) Write a generic static method swap that swaps two elements in an array at given indices.
(ii) Write a generic filter(List<T> list, Predicate<T> pred) method returning a new list of matching elements.
(iii) Explain why List<Dog> is not a subtype of List<Animal> even though Dog is a subtype of Animal (invariance of generics).

markscheme.text: static void swap(T[] arr, int i, int j) { T tmp=arr[i]; arr[i]=arr[j]; arr[j]=tmp; }
static List filter(List list, Predicate pred) { return list.stream().filter(pred).collect(Collectors.toList()); } or loop version

Invariance: if List were a subtype of List, you could write: List animals = new ArrayList(); animals.add(new Cat()); — adding a Cat to a Dog list! This would break type safety. Generics are invariant to prevent this

questions.title: Generic data structures
questions.prompt: This question concerns generic programming in Java.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Implement a generic immutable Optional class (simplified Java Optional) with factory methods of(T value) and empty(), and methods isPresent(), get(), orElse(T default), and map(Function f).

markscheme.text: Private constructor; static factory methods; private final T value (null for empty). isPresent, get (throws NoSuchElementException if empty)
orElse: return value != null ? value : default
map: Optional map(Function f) { return value==null ? empty() : of(f.apply(value)); } — type parameter U on method

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Implement a generic Result type representing either a success value or an error. Provide success(T), failure(E), isSuccess(), and fold(Function onSuccess, Function onFailure).

markscheme.text: Two static factory methods; private fields T value and E error; exactly one is non-null
fold: R fold(Function s, Function f) { return isSuccess() ? s.apply(value) : f.apply(error); }
Useful for railway-oriented programming: chains of operations that may fail, without exception propagation; all errors handled at the end via fold

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain why you cannot do new T[n] in Java generics. How does ArrayList work around this limitation?

markscheme.text: Cannot create generic arrays: type erasure means T is erased to Object at runtime; JVM doesn't know the type to allocate; worse, if allowed, you could break heap pollution

ArrayList workaround: internally stores Object[]; elements cast to E on retrieval. @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") E get(int i) { return (E) elementData[i]; }. The class maintains the invariant that only E objects are stored
Alternative: use Array.newInstance(Class<T> type, int size) with a passed-in Class<T> token; e.g., T[] arr = (T[]) Array.newInstance(clazz, n). Requires passing the Class<T> to the constructor

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is heap pollution in Java generics? Give an example and explain why @SafeVarargs is sometimes needed.

markscheme.text: Heap pollution: a variable of parameterised type refers to an object that is not of that type; causes ClassCastException at a seemingly safe cast

Example: static T[] toArray(T... args) { return args; }; String[] arr = toArray("a","b"); — at runtime, args is Object[] (varargs erased); assigning to String[] causes ArrayStoreException/ClassCastException

@SafeVarargs: annotation on method claiming it doesn't pollute heap through its varargs parameter. Suppresses compiler warning. Only safe if the method doesn't store anything into the varargs array or expose the array reference

questions.title: Tripos: generics and type system
questions.prompt: This question concerns Java's generic type system.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Implement a generic functional interface Function with method apply(A a). Add a default method andThen(Function f) for composition. Show how to create a pipeline of three functions.

markscheme.text: @FunctionalInterface Function<A,B>: B apply(A a);
andThen: default Function andThen(Function f) { return a -> f.apply(this.apply(a)); }
Pipeline: Function length = String::length; Function isEven = n -> n%2==0; Function display = b -> b ? "even" : "odd"; Function pipeline = length.andThen(isEven).andThen(display);

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Explain the difference between generic methods, generic classes, and generic interfaces. Give an example of each. When would you choose a generic method over a generic class?

markscheme.text: Generic class: type parameter on the class; all instances specialised to one type. class Box { T value; }
Generic interface: type parameter on interface; implementations specialise. interface Repository { T findById(ID id); }
Generic method: type parameter on the method; each call can use different type. static T identity(T x) { return x; }

Choose generic method when: operation independent of enclosing class's type (utility method); type parameter only needed for one method; allows type inference at call site without explicit parameterisation

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Compare Java generics (erasure-based) with C++ templates (expansion-based). Give two advantages and two disadvantages of Java's approach.

markscheme.text: Java erasure advantages: backward compatibility (generic code works with pre-1.5 code); smaller bytecode (one class, not one per type instantiation); simpler reflection model

Java erasure disadvantages: cannot create arrays of generic types; no specialisation for primitives (ArrayList<int> impossible — must use Integer); runtime type not available (can't do T instanceof Foo or new T())

C++ templates: one instantiation per type; specialise for performance; can use primitive types; but larger code size; longer compile times; complex error messages; templates are textual substitution not true generics

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What are raw types in Java? Why are they still permitted? Give an example showing how raw types can bypass generic type safety.

markscheme.text: Raw type: using a generic class without type parameters (e.g. List instead of List<String>). Permitted for backward compatibility with pre-generics Java code
Bypass: List rawList = new ArrayList(); rawList.add(42); String s = ((List)rawList).get(0); // ClassCastException

Raw types erase all generic type information; compiler gives unchecked warnings but allows the code. At runtime, casts fail. Best practice: never use raw types in new code; use <?> wildcard for truly unknown types

questions.title: Exception hierarchy
questions.prompt: Draw Java's exception hierarchy showing Throwable, Error, Exception, RuntimeException. Give one example of each concrete category. Which should application code typically catch?

markscheme.text: Throwable → Error (e.g. OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError) and Exception. Exception → RuntimeException (e.g. NullPointerException) and checked exceptions (e.g. IOException)

Application code should catch checked exceptions (and specific RuntimeExceptions when recoverable); never catch Error (unrecoverable JVM problems); avoid catching Exception or Throwable broadly as it hides bugs

questions.title: Multi-catch and finally
questions.prompt: Explain the purpose of finally in Java exception handling. Show multi-catch syntax (Java 7+). What is the difference between finally and try-with-resources? Is finally always executed?

markscheme.text: finally: block always executed after try (and any catch), whether or not an exception was thrown. Used for cleanup code (release resources)
Multi-catch: catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... } — handles multiple exception types with one handler; e is effectively final
try-with-resources: syntactic sugar for try-finally that closes AutoCloseable resources; handles suppressed exceptions correctly when both body and close() throw
finally not executed: if System.exit() is called; JVM crashes; thread is killed. Also: if Thread.stop() (deprecated) is called. Otherwise guaranteed

questions.title: Custom exceptions and exception chaining
questions.prompt: Design a custom exception hierarchy for a banking application: BankException (checked), InsufficientFundsException (subclass), AccountNotFoundException (subclass). Show exception chaining — catching a database SQLException and wrapping it in AccountNotFoundException.

markscheme.text: BankException extends Exception with constructors for message and cause. InsufficientFundsException and AccountNotFoundException extend BankException
Exception chaining: catch (SQLException e) { throw new AccountNotFoundException("Account not found", e); } — wraps low-level exception; getCause() returns the original
Benefit: abstraction — callers see BankException not SQL internals; full stack trace preserved for debugging via getCause()

questions.title: Exception handling patterns and anti-patterns

markscheme.text: Anti-pattern 1: swallowed exception (empty catch). Fix: at minimum log the exception; rethrow or handle appropriately. Silently ignoring exceptions masks bugs
Anti-pattern 2: using exception for control flow (returning -1 for invalid input). Better: validate input before parsing; return Optional<Integer>; or let the exception propagate if truly unexpected
Anti-pattern 3: double close — closing in catch and finally. Fix: use try-with-resources, which handles close exactly once and handles suppressed exceptions

questions.title: Robust error handling in systems
questions.prompt: This question concerns robust error handling.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators in Java. Which does ArrayList use? What exception does fail-fast throw, and when?
