markscheme.text: Fail-fast: detect concurrent modification during iteration (ConcurrentModificationException); tracks modCount; throws on detecting structural change. ArrayList, HashMap use fail-fast iterators
Fail-safe: iterates over a copy; modifications don't affect ongoing iteration; no exception. CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap use fail-safe. Trade-off: snapshot may be stale
ConcurrentModificationException is a best-effort check — not guaranteed to always detect concurrent modification; should not rely on it for correctness

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the Circuit Breaker pattern for fault tolerance. How does it help with cascading failures? Describe the three states (Closed, Open, Half-Open) and the transitions between them.

markscheme.text: Circuit Breaker: wraps calls to a fragile service; if the service fails repeatedly, the circuit 'trips' (opens) and subsequent calls fail fast without hitting the service, allowing it to recover
Closed: normal operation; calls pass through; failure count tracked. If failures exceed threshold → transition to Open
Open: all calls fail immediately without calling the service. After a timeout → transition to Half-Open
Half-Open: allow a limited number of test calls through. If they succeed → back to Closed; if they fail → back to Open

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is the principle of 'fail early, fail loudly'? How does it relate to assertions in Java (assert keyword)?

markscheme.text: Fail early: detect errors as close to their source as possible; don't propagate invalid state. Fail loudly: make failures obvious (throw exception, print diagnostic) rather than silently continuing

Java assert: assert condition : "message"; — throws AssertionError if condition is false; enabled with -ea JVM flag. Use for internal invariants, not user input validation. Disabled in production (no overhead)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Compare exception handling to functional error handling with Optional and Either/Result types. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each approach.

markscheme.text: Exceptions: advantage — propagate automatically through call stack; no boilerplate for intermediate callers. Disadvantage — invisible in type signatures (unchecked); can lead to unexpected program termination if not caught

Optional/Result: advantage — error handling visible in types; compiler forces caller to handle failure. Disadvantage — boilerplate for propagation (must explicitly flatMap/chain through every layer); cannot carry stack traces easily

Hybrid: use Optional for 'not found' (expected no-value); checked exceptions for recoverable errors; unchecked for programming errors. Java 19+ virtual threads and structured concurrency provide new patterns

questions.title: Tripos: testing and defensive code
questions.prompt: This question concerns robust software engineering.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe unit testing with JUnit 5. Write a test class for a Stack<Integer> class testing push, pop, and the behaviour when popping an empty stack.

markscheme.text: JUnit 5: @Test annotation; Assertions.assertEquals, assertThrows, assertTrue; @BeforeEach for setup; @DisplayName for readability
Tests: push then pop returns same value; push multiple, pop in LIFO order; size decrements on pop
Empty stack: assertThrows(EmptyStackException.class, () -> stack.pop())

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: What is test-driven development (TDD)? Describe the Red-Green-Refactor cycle. Give the first three test cases you would write for a FizzBuzz(int n) method (returns "Fizz" if divisible by 3, "Buzz" if by 5, "FizzBuzz" if by both, else the number as string).

markscheme.text: TDD: write a failing test first; write minimal code to make it pass; refactor. Red: test fails; Green: test passes; Refactor: clean up while keeping tests green
Test 1: fizzBuzz(1) = "1" (not divisible by 3 or 5)
Test 2: fizzBuzz(3) = "Fizz"; Test 3: fizzBuzz(5) = "Buzz"; further: fizzBuzz(15) = "FizzBuzz"

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is a mock object? How does Mockito enable testing of classes with external dependencies? Give a brief example of mocking a repository to test a service.

markscheme.text: Mock: a test double that simulates a dependency's behaviour without real implementation; verify interactions (was method called? with what args?)
Mockito: @Mock UserRepository mockRepo; when(mockRepo.findById(1)).thenReturn(Optional.of(user)); call service.getUser(1); verify(mockRepo).findById(1)
Benefit: isolates the unit under test from slow/external dependencies (database, network); tests run fast and deterministically

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is code coverage? Name two types of coverage metrics. Why is 100% coverage not sufficient to ensure correctness? What is mutation testing?

markscheme.text: Code coverage: percentage of code executed by tests. Metrics: line/statement coverage (each line executed); branch coverage (each if/else branch taken)
100% coverage not sufficient: tests may execute code without asserting anything (poor assertions); some bugs appear only for specific input values not tested; concurrent bugs not exercised

Mutation testing: automatically introduce small code changes (mutations e.g. > to >=, + to -); run tests. If no test fails, the mutation 'survives' — indicates a gap in test quality (tests don't detect that change). Measures test effectiveness, not just coverage

questions.title: Pattern categories
questions.prompt: Name the three categories of GoF (Gang of Four) design patterns. Give two examples from each category.

markscheme.text: Creational: manage object creation. Examples: Singleton, Factory Method, Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype
Structural: compose classes/objects. Examples: Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Proxy, Flyweight
Behavioural: communication between objects. Examples: Observer, Strategy, Template Method, Iterator, Command, State, Chain of Responsibility, Visitor

questions.title: Singleton pattern
questions.prompt: Implement the Singleton pattern in Java (thread-safe). Explain two problems with Singleton and when it is appropriate. Show the double-checked locking implementation.

markscheme.text: Double-checked locking: private static volatile Singleton instance; public static Singleton getInstance() { if(instance==null) { synchronized(Singleton.class) { if(instance==null) { instance=new Singleton(); } } } return instance; }
Problems: global state (hidden dependencies, makes testing hard); difficult to mock; violates SRP (manages own lifecycle). Appropriate: truly global resources (Logger, Configuration, thread pool)

questions.title: Decorator pattern
questions.prompt: Describe the Decorator pattern. Implement a coffee ordering system: base Coffee interface with cost() and description(); concrete EspressoBean class; MilkDecorator and SugarDecorator wrappers. Show ordering a double sugar latte.

markscheme.text: Decorator: wraps an object to add behaviour dynamically; alternative to subclassing for extending functionality. Each decorator implements the same interface and holds a reference to the wrapped object
Coffee interface; Espresso implements Coffee; MilkDecorator(Coffee c): cost() = c.cost()+0.5, description() = c.description()+", milk"
Double sugar latte: new SugarDecorator(new SugarDecorator(new MilkDecorator(new Espresso()))). Each layer wraps the previous, adding to cost and description

questions.title: Facade and Adapter patterns
questions.prompt:
(i) Describe the Facade pattern and give a use case.
(ii) Describe the Adapter pattern. Show how to adapt a legacy class OldPrinter (with method legacyPrint(String s)) to a modern Printer interface (with method print(String s)).
(iii) What is the difference between Adapter and Facade?

markscheme.text: Facade: provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. Use case: home theatre system with separate DVD, amplifier, projector — Facade provides watchMovie()/stopMovie() that orchestrates all components

Adapter: converts interface of one class to another. OldPrinterAdapter implements Printer; holds OldPrinter reference; print(s) calls oldPrinter.legacyPrint(s). Two types: class adapter (extends old class) and object adapter (composes)
Difference: Adapter makes incompatible interfaces work together (interface conversion). Facade simplifies a complex interface (doesn't change interface, just wraps it)

questions.title: Command and Chain of Responsibility patterns
questions.prompt: This question concerns behavioural design patterns.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the Command pattern. Implement a simple text editor command system with an Undo stack. Commands: InsertCommand and DeleteCommand each implementing Command with execute() and undo().

markscheme.text: Command: encapsulates a request as an object; supports undo/redo, queuing, logging. Interface: void execute(); void undo()
InsertCommand: stores text and position; execute inserts; undo removes. DeleteCommand: stores deleted text; execute removes; undo reinserts
Editor: Deque<Command> undoStack; invokeCommand(Command c) calls c.execute() and pushes to stack; undo() pops and calls c.undo()

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the Chain of Responsibility pattern. Implement a logging system where handlers for DEBUG, INFO, and ERROR process messages at their level and optionally pass to the next handler.

markscheme.text: Chain of Responsibility: pass request along a chain of handlers; each handler decides to process or pass on. Decouples sender from specific receiver
abstract LogHandler with level, setNext, handle. handle(level, msg): if this.level <= level, process; if next != null, next.handle(level, msg)
Chain: debug.setNext(info.setNext(error)). DEBUG messages processed by all; ERROR only by ERROR handler. Client calls debug.handle(level, msg)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the State pattern. How does it differ from a switch statement? Implement a TrafficLight with states Red, Green, Yellow and transition logic.

markscheme.text: State: allow object to alter behaviour when internal state changes; each state is a class. Vs switch: state transitions encapsulated in state classes (OCP); no large switch/if-else; states can be added without modifying context
TrafficLight context holds State reference; State interface: next() returns next State, getColor(). RedState.next() = Green, GreenState.next() = Yellow, YellowState.next() = Red
Correct Java implementation; light.changeState() calls state = state.next(); demonstrating encapsulated transitions

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: How do modern Java features (lambdas, functional interfaces) reduce the need for some traditional design patterns? Give two examples of patterns that can be simplified.

markscheme.text: Strategy pattern: traditionally requires a strategy interface and multiple implementing classes. With lambdas: pass lambda directly as Comparator.compare or Runnable — no boilerplate class needed
Command pattern: Command interface is a functional interface; commands can be lambdas or method references. Undo still needs state (closures capture it)

Observer pattern: replaced/simplified by reactive streams (RxJava, Project Reactor) or CompletableFuture for asynchronous event handling; lambdas as callbacks. Also: Factory Method replaced by method references (constructor references Foo::new)

questions.title: Tripos: design patterns in practice
questions.prompt: This question concerns applying design patterns to real software.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Design a plugin system for an application using the Factory Method and Strategy patterns. The system should allow adding new file format handlers (CSV, JSON, XML) without modifying existing code.

markscheme.text: FileHandler interface: readFile(String path), writeFile(String path, Data data). CsvHandler, JsonHandler, XmlHandler implement FileHandler
Factory Method: FileHandlerFactory with createHandler(String format): return appropriate handler based on format string. Or registry pattern: Map<String, FileHandler> registered handlers
Strategy: application holds a FileHandler field; can be swapped at runtime. New format: implement FileHandler and register with factory — no existing code modified (OCP)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture. How do Observer and Strategy patterns appear in MVC? Give a concrete example.

markscheme.text: MVC: Model (data/business logic), View (presentation), Controller (handles input, updates model). Separation of concerns: UI can change without touching business logic
Observer in MVC: Model notifies registered Views when data changes (Observer pattern). Multiple views (table, chart) can display the same model data, auto-updating
Strategy in MVC: Controller can swap strategies for different user actions or input sources. Example: same Model displayed by different View strategies (web view vs desktop view)
Concrete example: stock price model; two views (chart and table) register as observers; controller handles buy/sell actions; model updates and notifies views

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What is an anti-pattern? Describe two common OOP anti-patterns (God Object and Spaghetti Code) and how to refactor them.

markscheme.text: Anti-pattern: a common 'solution' that is actually harmful or counter-productive. God Object: one class knows/does everything; low cohesion, high coupling. Refactor: apply SRP; split into multiple focused classes
Spaghetti code: tangled control flow; deeply nested ifs, no clear structure; business logic mixed with presentation. Refactor: extract methods; apply strategy/template method; separate concerns

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe the Flyweight pattern. When should it be used? Give an example from Java's standard library or a game engine.

markscheme.text: Flyweight: share instances of commonly-used objects to reduce memory. Intrinsic state (shared) stored in flyweight; extrinsic state (context-specific) passed by client

Java examples: Integer.valueOf(-128 to 127) returns cached instances; String interning (string pool); Character flyweights. Game: each tree in a forest shares a TreeType flyweight (mesh, texture) with extrinsic position/scale per instance
Use when: many objects of similar type; high memory usage; most state can be extrinsic. Trade-off: more complex client code (must supply extrinsic state); adds factory indirection

questions.title: Lambda syntax
questions.prompt: What is a lambda expression in Java? Write lambda expressions for: (i) a Runnable that prints "Hello"; (ii) a Comparator<String> that compares by length; (iii) a Predicate<Integer> that tests if a number is positive.

markscheme.text: (i) () -> System.out.println("Hello")
(ii) (s1, s2) -> Integer.compare(s1.length(), s2.length()) or Comparator.comparingInt(String::length)
(iii) n -> n > 0

questions.title: Method references
questions.prompt: Explain the four kinds of method references in Java. Give an example of each. Which functional interface does each correspond to?

markscheme.text: Static method: Integer::parseInt — equivalent to x -> Integer.parseInt(x); corresponds to Function
Instance method of particular object: System.out::println — equivalent to x -> System.out.println(x); Consumer
Instance method of arbitrary object of a type: String::length — equivalent to s -> s.length(); ToIntFunction
Constructor: ArrayList::new — equivalent to () -> new ArrayList<>(); Supplier

questions.title: Stream operations
questions.prompt: Given a List<String> names containing ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Dave", "Eve"], write stream operations to: (i) filter names longer than 3 characters; (ii) convert all to uppercase; (iii) sort alphabetically; (iv) collect to a List. Combine all four into one stream pipeline.

markscheme.text: Individual operations: filter(n -> n.length()>3); map(String::toUpperCase); sorted(); collect(Collectors.toList())
Combined pipeline: names.stream().filter(n -> n.length()>3).map(String::toUpperCase).sorted().collect(Collectors.toList())
Result: ["ALICE", "CHARLIE", "DAVE"] — Bob (3 chars) excluded; Eve (3 chars) excluded; sorted alphabetically

questions.title: Reduce, flatMap, and collectors
questions.prompt:
(i) Explain the difference between map and flatMap on streams. Give an example where flatMap is needed.
(ii) Write a stream pipeline that takes a list of sentences (List), splits each into words, counts total word frequency using Collectors.groupingBy.
(iii) What is the difference between reduce and collect?

markscheme.text: map: 1-to-1 transformation. flatMap: 1-to-many (flattens the resulting streams). Example: sentences.stream().flatMap(s -> Arrays.stream(s.split(" "))) — each sentence → stream of words, all flattened into one stream
Word frequency: sentences.stream().flatMap(s -> Arrays.stream(s.split(" "))).collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Function.identity(), Collectors.counting())) → Map

reduce: combines elements into a single value using an associative binary function (e.g. sum). collect: mutable reduction into a container (e.g. List, Map, StringBuilder) — more efficient for building mutable results

questions.title: Advanced streams and parallel processing
questions.prompt: This question concerns advanced Stream operations.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe lazy evaluation in Java streams. How does it differ from eager evaluation? Give an example where laziness avoids unnecessary computation using findFirst() with filter().

markscheme.text: Lazy: intermediate operations (filter, map) don't execute until a terminal operation is called. Elements are processed one at a time through the pipeline
findFirst(): stops as soon as the first matching element is found — doesn't process remaining elements. Eager would process all elements first
Example: IntStream.range(0, 1000000).filter(n -> n%2==0).findFirst() — with lazy evaluation, processes only 2 elements (0,1); eager would filter all million

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: What is a parallel stream? When is it beneficial? What are the requirements for operations to be safely parallelised?

markscheme.text: Parallel stream: splits data into chunks processed by multiple threads (ForkJoinPool). Use when: large datasets; operations are CPU-intensive and independent

Requirements: stateless operations (no shared mutable state); associative and non-interfering operations for reduce/collect. Avoid: shared mutation in lambdas, ordering guarantees (use forEachOrdered if needed)
Not always beneficial: thread overhead dominates for small datasets; I/O-bound operations don't improve; parallel stream's ForkJoinPool is shared — avoid blocking operations

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Write a stream pipeline to process a list of orders, where each Order has fields: customer (String), amount (double), and category (String). Find the top 3 customers by total spend, using Collectors.groupingBy and Collectors.summingDouble.

markscheme.text: orders.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Order::getCustomer, Collectors.summingDouble(Order::getAmount))) → Map
Sort by value descending: .entrySet().stream().sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByValue().reversed())
.limit(3).map(Map.Entry::getKey).collect(Collectors.toList()) — top 3 customers. Complete pipeline correct

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe Optional's stream integration (Java 9+). What does Optional.stream() return? Show how to filter out empty Optionals from a List> using flatMap.

markscheme.text: Optional.stream(): returns a Stream of 0 or 1 elements (empty stream if Optional.empty(), stream of the value otherwise)

Filter empties: optionals.stream().flatMap(Optional::stream).collect(Collectors.toList()) — each Optional contributes 0 or 1 elements; flatMap flattens. Equivalent to: optionals.stream().filter(Optional::isPresent).map(Optional::get)
Java 9+ approach (Optional::stream) is cleaner and avoids calling .get() which could theoretically throw if logic changes

questions.title: Tripos: functional programming in Java
questions.prompt: This question concerns functional programming features in Java.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Compare Java's Stream API with OCaml's List module functions (map, filter, fold). What are the key design differences? Why does OCaml not need a concept of lazy streams for finite lists?

markscheme.text: Both provide map/filter/fold; Java Streams are lazy (pipeline materialised on terminal op) and can represent infinite sequences; OCaml list functions are eager (process all elements immediately)
Java adds: parallel processing; mutable collections; primitive streams (IntStream); collectors for mutable reduction. OCaml list functions return new lists (immutable); simpler mental model

OCaml finite lists: already in memory; no benefit from lazy evaluation. Java's laziness is needed because streams can be backed by I/O, infinite sequences, or large datasets that shouldn't be fully materialised

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Implement a simple monad-like pipeline in Java using lambdas. Define a Maybe (like Optional) with flatMap(Function>). Demonstrate it with a chain of operations that could fail (parse int, then compute square root, then convert to string).

markscheme.text: Maybe: holds Optional; static of(T), empty(). flatMap: Maybe flatMap(Function> f) { return value.map(f).orElseGet(Maybe::empty); }

Pipeline: Maybe.of(input).flatMap(s -> { try { return Maybe.of(Integer.parseInt(s)); } catch(...) { return Maybe.empty(); } }).flatMap(n -> n >= 0 ? Maybe.of(Math.sqrt(n)) : Maybe.empty()).flatMap(d -> Maybe.of(d.toString()))
Demonstrates monadic composition: each step only runs if previous succeeded; failure propagates cleanly without exceptions in the main chain

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain the difference between external and internal iteration. How do Java streams (internal) compare to traditional for loops (external) in terms of parallelisation and abstraction?

markscheme.text: External iteration: programmer controls the loop (for/while); specifies how to iterate. Internal iteration: pass a function to the collection; the collection controls the loop

Parallelisation: external for-loop hard to parallelise (programmer must manage threads). Internal (streams): call .parallel() and the library handles threading — abstraction allows optimisation without code change
Abstraction level: internal iteration expresses what to do, not how; more declarative; enables chain of operations without intermediate collections; library can optimise ordering/fusion of operations

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is a closure in Java (in the context of lambdas)? What limitation does Java impose on captured variables? Why does this restriction exist?

markscheme.text: Closure: a lambda captures variables from its enclosing scope. Captured variables must be effectively final (not reassigned after capture)
Restriction example: int x = 5; Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(x); — legal. x = 6; Runnable r = ... — compile error (x not effectively final)

Why: lambdas can be executed in different threads or at different times. If x were mutable, reading it later could give a different value — race condition or stale value. Effectively-final restriction ensures the captured value is stable. Contrast: OCaml closures can capture mutable refs explicitly
